text:
Romanian Deadlift (RDL) — Setup and Execution

Starting position: Stand with feet hip-width apart, barbell over mid-foot. Grip the bar just outside the legs with a double-overhand grip. The bar should remain in contact with the body throughout the lift.

Hip hinge initiation: Push the hips back — not down. The distinction is critical: the RDL is a hinge, not a squat. Keep a slight bend in the knees (15–20 degrees) and maintain that angle throughout; the knees do not track forward over the toes. Think of the hips as the hinge point and the torso as the lever arm rotating around it.

Back position: Maintain a neutral spine from head to tailbone — no lumbar flexion at the bottom. The chest stays tall, shoulders retracted and depressed (scapulae pulled back and down). Brace the core as if absorbing a punch before each rep.

Bar path: The barbell drags along the thighs as you lower it. It should stay within 2 cm of the body the entire way down. Letting it drift forward dramatically increases the moment arm on the lumbar spine.

Range of motion: Lower until you feel a strong hamstring stretch — typically to mid-shin for most athletes. The limiting factor is hamstring flexibility, not a fixed depth. Never sacrifice neutral spine to reach lower.

Drive phase: Drive the hips forward to return to standing. Squeeze the glutes at lockout. Do not hyperextend the lumbar at the top.

Romanian Deadlift — Muscles Worked and Common Errors

Primary movers: Hamstrings (biceps femoris, semitendinosus, semimembranosus) are the prime movers — they decelerate hip flexion on the way down and produce hip extension on the way up. Gluteus maximus contributes significantly at the top of the movement.

Secondary: Erector spinae (isometric stabilizer), adductor magnus (contributes to hip extension), gastrocnemius (minor).

Common errors:
1. Squatting the weight — knees drifting forward converts an RDL into a hybrid squat-deadlift, offloading the hamstrings. Fix: keep shins nearly vertical, push hips back first.
2. Rounding the lower back — usually caused by insufficient hamstring flexibility or loading too heavy before mobility is adequate. Fix: reduce range of motion until flexibility improves, or elevate the plates on blocks.
3. Bar drifting away from the body — dramatically increases lower back stress. Fix: maintain active lat engagement (think: protect your armpits) to keep the bar close.
4. Hyperextending at lockout — compresses lumbar facets. Fix: stop when hips and knees are fully extended and glutes are squeezed; no lean-back.
5. Looking up excessively — creates cervical strain and often leads to lumbar overextension. Fix: keep gaze neutral, roughly 10 feet ahead on the floor.

Loading progression: The RDL is typically trained in the 3–4 sets of 6–10 rep range for hypertrophy, or 3–5 sets of 4–6 for strength. It responds well to slow eccentrics (3–4 second lowering phase) to increase time under tension on the hamstrings.

Romanian Deadlift — ACE Exercise Library

The Romanian Deadlift is one of the most effective exercises for developing posterior chain strength and hamstring hypertrophy. It is distinct from the conventional deadlift in that the bar does not touch the floor between reps, maintaining continuous tension on the hamstrings.

Stance width: Hip-width is standard. A narrower stance (feet nearly together) increases the stretch on the inner hamstrings (semitendinosus and semimembranosus). A slightly wider stance shifts emphasis toward the outer hamstring (biceps femoris long head) and adductor magnus. Experiment within a few inches either way — there is no universally correct width.

Grip options: Double-overhand is standard and sufficient for most training loads. A mixed grip (one supinated) is sometimes used for heavier loads but is not recommended for the RDL since the asymmetry can cause uneven torso rotation under fatigue. Straps are appropriate when grip becomes the limiting factor before the target muscles are adequately fatigued.

Breathing and bracing: Inhale and brace the core (Valsalva maneuver) before initiating the hinge. Exhale at or just after the sticking point on the way up. Do not exhale at the bottom of the movement, as this releases intra-abdominal pressure at the point of highest spinal loading.

Programming note: The RDL is complementary to squatting patterns and should be included in any program targeting lower body hypertrophy or athletic performance. It is commonly programmed as an accessory to the conventional deadlift or as a primary posterior chain movement in its own right.
